Now, let's code this in Java. The code is as follows:

class Solution {
    public int brokenCalc(int startValue, int target) {
        int operations = 0;
        while (target > startValue) {
            if (target % 2 == 1) {
                target++;
                operations++;
            }
            target /= 2;
            operations++;
        }
        return operations + (startValue - target);
    }
}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int brokenCalc(int startValue, int target) {
        int operations = 0;
        while (target > startValue) {
            if (target % 2 == 1) {
                target++;
                operations++;
            }
            target /= 2;
            operations++;
        }
        return operations + (startValue - target);
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(log(target))
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution works backwards from the target to the startValue. When the target is greater than startValue, it either increments (if odd) or divides by 2 (if even), counting operations. Once the target is less than or equal to startValue, it adds the remaining subtraction steps. This greedy approach ensures minimal operations by prioritizing division over incrementing when possible.
